title: Sweet Auburn Black Enterprise & Civil Rights
summary: Follow Sweet Auburn through Black church life, civil-rights memory, archives, enterprise, and the institutions that gave Atlanta one of its defining historic corridors.

stops.title: Martin Luther King, Jr. National Historical Park
stops.description: Open the route inside the King district, where Sweet Auburn reads as both neighborhood ground and national freedom terrain.

stops.title: Ebenezer Baptist Church
stops.description: Anchor the route in Black church life, ministry, and movement leadership at one of Atlanta's most consequential sacred spaces.

stops.title: The King Center
stops.description: Move from neighborhood history into movement memory at the institution that carries King's public legacy into the present.

stops.title: Auburn Avenue Research Library
stops.description: Read Sweet Auburn through archives, stewardship, and the long preservation of Black Atlanta's intellectual life.

stops.title: Big Bethel A.M.E. Church
stops.description: Expand the route beyond the King family orbit into the wider church and civic infrastructure that shaped Auburn Avenue.

stops.title: Prince Hall Masonic Temple
stops.description: Close with Black enterprise and fraternal power at a site that helps Sweet Auburn feel broader than one movement story.

title: Atlanta University Center & Black Intellectual Capital
summary: Trace Black intellectual life through the Atlanta University Center's shared library, historic campuses, medical training, and the institutions that shaped national leadership.

stops.title: Robert W. Woodruff Library
stops.description: Begin at the shared intellectual commons of the AUC, where archives, scholarship, and institutional memory meet.

stops.title: Clark Atlanta University
stops.description: Set the route inside the consortium's academic scale at a campus that ties Black professional formation to Atlanta's civic life.

stops.title: Morehouse College
stops.description: Move into one of the city's defining institutions for Black leadership, scholarship, ministry, and public voice.

stops.title: Spelman College
stops.description: Center Black women's intellectual leadership at a campus that is indispensable to any honest reading of Atlanta's educational legacy.

stops.title: Morehouse School of Medicine
stops.description: Close with Black medical training, public service, and institutional ambition in a place where scholarship becomes care infrastructure.

title: Civil Rights, Public Power & National Memory
summary: Move between movement memory and governing power through Atlanta's civil-rights institutions, city leadership spaces, and the landmarks that turned local struggle into national history.

stops.title: National Center for Civil and Human Rights
stops.description: Open at Atlanta's most public-facing civil-rights institution, where local movement history is framed inside a national freedom narrative.

stops.title: Atlanta City Hall
stops.description: Read public power at the city scale, where governance, executive leadership, and local policy decisions shape the life of Atlanta.

stops.title: Georgia State Capitol
stops.description: Add state power, protest geography, and legal consequence to the route at one of Georgia's most visible political sites.

stops.title: The King Center
stops.description: Return to movement stewardship and public ritual at the institution that carries King's legacy into Atlanta's present civic life.

stops.title: Martin Luther King, Jr. National Historical Park
stops.description: Close where neighborhood ground and national memory meet, tying public power back to the lived geography of Black Atlanta.

title: Black Atlanta Arts, Music & Contemporary Culture
summary: Trace Black Atlanta's creative life through museums, trap-era culture, public art districts, visual art, and the moving landscape of the BeltLine.

stops.title: APEX Museum
stops.description: Open with a museum that roots Black Atlanta's present-day creativity in the longer social and cultural life of Sweet Auburn.

stops.title: Trap Music Museum
stops.description: Move into Atlanta's modern sound at the route's clearest contemporary music anchor, where trap becomes public cultural history.

stops.title: Black Music & Entertainment Walk of Fame District
stops.description: Hold Atlanta's public music legacy in the downtown entertainment district where the city honors Black artists as part of its living cultural identity.

stops.title: Hammonds House Museum
stops.description: Bring in Black visual art, curatorial depth, and West End cultural life at one of the city's strongest arts institutions.

stops.title: Atlanta BeltLine Westside Trail
stops.description: Close with a public-art and public-space corridor where contemporary culture, redevelopment, and city movement all become visible at once.

title: West End, BeltLine & Neighborhood Change
summary: Read historic West End through neighborhood memory, Black cultural institutions, and the BeltLine corridor where preservation, public space, and redevelopment meet closer to home.

stops.title: West End Historic District
stops.description: Open with the neighborhood itself, using West End's preservation history to frame the route around memory, infrastructure, and change over time.

stops.title: Hammonds House Museum
stops.description: Anchor the route in Black visual art and West End cultural life at one of the city's strongest neighborhood-scale arts institutions.

stops.title: The Wren's Nest
stops.description: Add long neighborhood texture and literary memory at a landmark that helps West End read as a layered historic district rather than a single theme.

stops.title: Shrine of the Black Madonna
stops.description: Bring in Black institutional and spiritual life through a West End anchor that connects neighborhood identity to broader cultural and political traditions.

stops.title: Atlanta BeltLine Southwest Trail
stops.description: Close where neighborhood memory meets redevelopment pressure, public art, and new city movement on the BeltLine segment that actually borders West End.
